metadata.title: Maharashtra Traffic Sector — Actionable Reference Data
metadata.description: Helplines, portals, and contact details extracted from official government websites for the traffic sector in Maharashtra

grievance_mechanisms.title: Challenge a Wrong E-Challan Online
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you receive a traffic e-challan that is incorrect — wrong vehicle, wrong location, wrong violation, duplicate challan, or amount mismatch
steps: Step 1: Visit mahatrafficechallan.gov.in/MH-Echallan-Grievance
Step 2: Select 'Apply Grievance Against Challan' or 'Apply Grievance Against Receipt'
Step 3: Enter your challan number or vehicle registration number
Step 4: Fill in the grievance details explaining why the challan is wrong
Step 5: Upload supporting documents (RC copy, photos, dashcam footage, GPS data) — max 2MB per file in PDF format
Step 6: Submit and note down your grievance reference number
Step 7: Track status using the reference number on the same portal
required_documents: Challan number or vehicle registration number
Evidence proving challan is wrong (photos, GPS logs, dashcam, toll receipts, parking slips, CCTV footage)
grievance_mechanisms.resolution_timeline: 30 working days (as per Maharashtra Public Grievance Redressal Act 2015)

grievance_mechanisms.title: Challenge a Wrong Challan via Mumbai Traffic Police App
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you receive a wrong e-challan in Mumbai specifically and want to use the mobile app
grievance_mechanisms.app_availability: Available on Google Play Store and Apple App Store — search 'Mumbai Traffic Police'
steps: Step 1: Download the Mumbai Traffic Police app from Play Store or Apple Store
Step 2: Register with your mobile number and verify with OTP
Step 3: Go to 'My E-challans' to check if challan shows as unpaid with image proof
Step 4: Go back to main page and tap 'Grievance'
Step 5: Tap the + sign at bottom right
Step 6: Fill in details and upload proof
Step 7: Submit — resolution typically within 48 hours for clear cases

grievance_mechanisms.title: Escalate via Aaple Sarkar (State Grievance Portal)
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When your e-challan grievance was rejected or not resolved within 30 days, or for any traffic-related complaint against a government department
grievance_mechanisms.portal_name: Aaple Sarkar — Integrated Grievance Redressal System (IGRS)
steps: Step 1: Visit aaplesarkar.mahaonline.gov.in
Step 2: Register as 'Citizen' with full name (as per Aadhaar), district, mobile number
Step 3: Verify mobile with OTP
Step 4: Complete profile with address and contact details
Step 5: Read and check all mandatory checklist items before filing
Step 6: For traffic challans — file under 'Transport Department' or 'Traffic Police' at 'District Level'
Step 7: Fill grievance form with clear description and upload documents
Step 8: Submit — you will receive a token number
Step 9: Track using token number — competent authority must resolve within 21 days
Step 10: After resolution, provide feedback via 'Satisfied' / 'Dissatisfied' option
important_notes: Individual challan disputes are DISTRICT-level matters
State-level grievances are for policy issues affecting multiple people
Cannot save form as draft — have all documents ready before starting
Do not file the same complaint on multiple portals
One-time registration works for all future grievances

grievance_mechanisms.title: File RTI to Get Challan Evidence
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you want to demand the evidence used to issue your challan — camera calibration certificate, officer details, surveillance logs — especially useful if your grievance was rejected without showing evidence
grievance_mechanisms.fee: Rs. 10 (Court Fee Stamp for offline, online payment for digital)
steps: Step 1: Identify the correct department — for traffic challans, address to 'Transport Department' or 'Traffic Police' of your district
Step 2: Visit rtionline.maharashtra.gov.in for online filing, or write a physical application
Step 3: Write 'Application under RTI Act 2005' at the top
Step 4: Address to 'Public Information Officer' of the relevant department
Step 5: Ask specific, direct questions (see sample questions below)
Step 6: Pay Rs. 10 fee
Step 7: Submit online or send via Speed Post / Registered Post
Step 8: PIO must respond within 30 days
Step 9: If no response or unsatisfactory response — file First Appeal (no fee) within 30 days
sample_questions_for_traffic_challan: Please provide the photographic/video evidence used to issue challan number [XXXXX] dated [DATE] against vehicle [REG NUMBER]
Please provide the calibration certificate and last maintenance date of the speed camera/CCTV at location [LOCATION] that captured the alleged violation
Please provide the name, badge number, and duty roster of the officer who issued challan number [XXXXX]
How many challans issued by the same camera/officer at the same location have been cancelled in the last 12 months?
Please provide the Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) followed for issuing e-challans in this jurisdiction
grievance_mechanisms.language: Can be filed in English or Marathi
grievance_mechanisms.source: Government of Maharashtra RTI Portal — rtionline.maharashtra.gov.in

grievance_mechanisms.title: Report Traffic Police Bribery to ACB
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When a traffic police officer demands a bribe — whether for not issuing a challan, for reducing fine amount, for not impounding vehicle, or for any other reason

complaint_channels.detail: ACB Maharashtra app on Play Store

steps: Step 1: DO NOT pay the bribe. Stay calm.
Step 2: If safe, discreetly record audio/video of the bribe demand using your phone
Step 3: Note the officer's badge number, name (if visible), location, date, and time
Step 4: Call ACB toll-free 1064 immediately, or WhatsApp +91-9930997700
Step 5: Alternatively, go to acbmaharashtra.gov.in → 'Bribe Demand Complaint'
Step 6: Fill the form with all details (only mobile number is compulsory)
Step 7: Attach evidence — images, audio clips, or videos
Step 8: ACB may arrange a 'trap' — you will need to be directly involved
Step 9: Trap money is returned to complainant after 30 days
Step 10: Your identity is kept secret except in trap complaints
important_notes: Trap complaint can only be made against the public servant with whom your legal work is pending
You must attend the ACB office in person for a trap operation
If accused threatens you after complaint, ACB collects evidence and applies for cancellation of accused's bail
ACB investigates three types of crimes: Trap, DPA (Disproportionate Assets), and Criminal Misconduct
For on-the-spot bribery (like traffic constable demanding money), collect video/audio evidence and upload to ACB portal
grievance_mechanisms.legal_basis: Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988 — Section 7 (bribery by public servant)

grievance_mechanisms.title: Complain Against Police Misconduct to PCA
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When a traffic police officer engages in serious misconduct — use of excessive force, illegal detention, extortion, harassment, abuse of authority, or causing grievous hurt
grievance_mechanisms.legal_basis: Section 22P, Maharashtra Police (Amendment and Continuance) Act, 2014

state_pca.jurisdiction: Complaints against SENIOR police officers (above rank of Senior PI)
state_pca.address: Cooperage Telephone Exchange, 4th Floor, Maharshi Karve Road, Nariman Point, Mumbai 400021

divisional_pcas.jurisdiction: Complaints against police officers up to rank of Senior Police Inspector

who_can_file: Anyone on behalf of the victim (with victim's consent affidavit)
what_you_can_complain_about: Rape or sexual assault by police personnel
Any serious violation of law or abuse of authority
steps: Step 1: Determine officer rank to choose State PCA or Divisional PCA
Step 2: Write complaint with full details — date, time, location, officer identity, what happened
Step 3: Attach all evidence — medical reports, photos, videos, witness statements
Step 4: Submit to appropriate PCA office (in person or by post)
Step 5: PCA will issue written order acknowledging the complaint (free of cost)
Step 6: PCA issues notice to the accused officer and provides them a copy of your complaint
Step 7: PCA conducts inquiry — may use its own investigating officer or external agency
Step 8: You will be informed of the outcome
grievance_mechanisms.source: CHRI Maharashtra PCA User Guide 2019 — humanrightsinitiative.org

grievance_mechanisms.title: Verify if a Challan is Genuine (Anti-Scam Check)
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you receive an SMS or email about a traffic challan and want to verify if it is genuine before paying — fake challan scams are increasing
grievance_mechanisms.portal_name: Parivahan Sewa (Ministry of Road Transport & Highways)
steps: Step 1: Visit parivahan.gov.in
Step 2: Click 'Check Online Services'
Step 3: Select 'Check Challan Status'
Step 4: Enter your vehicle registration number
Step 5: Enter chassis number (last 5 digits)
Step 6: View all challans against your vehicle
Step 7: Cross-verify challan number, date, and amount with what you received via SMS/email
Step 8: If the challan does NOT appear on Parivahan, it is likely FAKE — report to cybercrime.gov.in
grievance_mechanisms.alternate_verification: Maharashtra E-Challan Portal — https://mahatrafficechallan.gov.in/ (enter vehicle number + last 4 digits of engine/chassis number)
grievance_mechanisms.source: Ministry of Road Transport & Highways — parivahan.gov.in

grievance_mechanisms.title: Challenge Challan in Court or Lok Adalat
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When online grievance is rejected, RTI reveals procedural errors, or for non-compoundable offences that require court appearance

options.when: For manual challans or when online grievance was unjustly rejected
steps: Step 1: Collect all evidence — dashcam footage, GPS logs, parking slips, RTI responses
Step 2: File a written appeal to the relevant traffic court within 15-30 days of challan
Step 3: Present your case before the traffic magistrate
Step 4: Magistrate examines evidence and gives ruling

options.when: For e-challans above Rs. 5000 where you have strong evidence
options.note: Cost-benefit favours this route for challans above Rs. 5000

options.when: For settling challans with reduced fines — no court fees, no lawyers needed
steps: Step 1: Check NALSA website (nalsa.gov.in) for next Lok Adalat dates
Step 2: Attend with all challan documents
Step 3: Cases are settled with mutual agreement, often with reduced fines

options.when: When there is abuse of power, mala fide intention, or fundamental rights violation by traffic authorities
steps: Step 1: Consult a lawyer — writ petitions require legal expertise
Step 2: File before the High Court under Article 226 of the Constitution
Step 3: Seek quashing of the challan
options.note: This is a last resort for arbitrary or mass challans where fundamental rights are violated

important_citizen_rights: You cannot be forced to pay a challan without being shown evidence
You cannot be denied the right to challenge the evidence
You cannot be ignored when you file a legitimate grievance
Always ask for a receipt for any fine paid
Never sign a challan or admit guilt unless you are sure
Preserve all communication, photos, videos, and vehicle logs
legal_basis: Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 — Section 207 (power of court to make orders)
Constitution of India — Article 226 (writ jurisdiction of High Courts)
Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 (Lok Adalat provisions)
grievance_mechanisms.source: Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Constitution of India, NALSA guidelines

grievance_mechanisms.title: Vehicle Towed Without Valid Reason
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When your vehicle has been towed by traffic police without valid reason, or towed from a location where parking was not clearly prohibited, or towed without following proper procedure
steps: Step 1: Note the exact location, time, and any signage (or lack of no-parking signs) — take photos if possible
Step 2: Go to the nearest traffic police station or the towing yard to locate your vehicle
Step 3: Demand a towing receipt — police are required to issue one with details of the offence, vehicle number, towing charges, and the officer's details
Step 4: Pay the towing charges to retrieve your vehicle (you can challenge later — retrieve first)
Step 5: If you believe the towing was unjust — check whether no-parking signs were clearly visible at the location
Step 6: File a grievance on the Maharashtra E-Challan Grievance Portal (mahatrafficechallan.gov.in/MH-Echallan-Grievance) with photos showing no signage
Step 7: File an RTI asking for the no-parking notification/order for that specific location — if none exists, the towing was unauthorized
Step 8: If towing caused damage to your vehicle, document it with photos and file a compensation claim
Step 9: For persistent issues, approach the traffic court or file on Aaple Sarkar portal
important_notes: Under Section 127 MV Act, towing is only authorized if the vehicle is left unattended for 10+ hours, parked in a legally prohibited zone, or creating a traffic hazard
Only a police officer IN UNIFORM with jurisdiction can authorize towing
The owner is responsible for towing costs ONLY if the towing was legally authorized
If no-parking signs were not clearly displayed, the towing may be challengeable
Always get a receipt — towing without a receipt is an irregularity you can report
Towing fine in Maharashtra: Rs. 500 (first offence), Rs. 1,500 (repeat)
grievance_mechanisms.legal_basis: Motor Vehicles Act 1988 — Section 122, 127; Maharashtra MV Rules 1989
grievance_mechanisms.source: Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Maharashtra Traffic Police

grievance_mechanisms.title: What to Do Immediately After a Road Accident
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you are involved in or witness a road accident — these are the first steps to take before anything else
steps: Step 1: STOP the vehicle immediately — do not flee the scene (leaving is a criminal offence under BNS Section 106(2) with up to 10 years imprisonment)
Step 2: Call 112 (Maharashtra Emergency Response System — MERS) for police, ambulance, and fire
Step 3: Help the injured — take them to the nearest hospital. Under the Good Samaritan Law (MV Act Section 134A), you are legally protected from harassment when helping accident victims
Step 4: Do NOT move vehicles from the accident position unless they are blocking emergency access — the accident scene is evidence
Step 5: Take photos/videos of the accident scene, vehicle positions, damage, injuries, skid marks, road conditions, and any traffic signals
Step 6: Collect contact details and vehicle numbers of all parties involved and witnesses
Step 7: Go to the nearest police station and file an FIR — or call 112 and the police will come to the scene
Step 8: Get a MLC (Medico-Legal Certificate) from the hospital — this is crucial evidence
Step 9: Inform your insurance company within 24 hours
Step 10: Keep all medical bills, treatment records, FIR copy, and photographs safely — you will need these for insurance or MACT claims
important_notes: Good Samaritans who help accident victims are protected by law — no police harassment, no unnecessary questioning, no mandatory court appearance
Golden Hour Scheme: Cashless treatment up to Rs. 1.5 lakh is available for accident victims at designated hospitals during the first hour
Police must submit Accident Information Report to MACT within 30 days of FIR
There is NO time limitation for filing accident compensation claims before MACT
Even if you were partially at fault, you can still claim compensation — amount is reduced proportionally
grievance_mechanisms.legal_basis: Motor Vehicles Act 1988 — Sections 134, 134A (Good Samaritan), 161-163A (compensation), 166 (MACT claims); BNS Section 106(2) (hit and run)
grievance_mechanisms.source: Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Maharashtra Emergency Response System

grievance_mechanisms.title: Claim Compensation as Road Accident Victim (MACT)
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you or a family member has been injured or killed in a road accident and you want to claim compensation from the at-fault driver's insurance company
steps: Step 1: Ensure an FIR has been filed at the police station where the accident occurred
Step 2: Collect all documents — FIR copy, MLC/post-mortem report, medical bills, treatment records, disability certificate (if applicable), income proof, age proof, vehicle insurance details
Step 3: File a claim petition before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) — you can file at the place of accident, your residence, or where the vehicle is registered
Step 4: No court fee is required for filing MACT claims
Step 5: MACT sends notices to all parties — driver, vehicle owner, and insurance company
Step 6: Both sides present evidence and witnesses during hearings
Step 7: MACT determines compensation based on — severity of injury, medical expenses, loss of income, disability, pain and suffering, age of victim
Step 8: Insurance company must pay the awarded compensation within 30 days
Step 9: If unsatisfied with the award, appeal to the High Court within 90 days
who_can_file: Legal representatives of the deceased (spouse, children, parents)
An authorized agent on behalf of the victim
required_documents: All medical bills and treatment records
Proof of income (salary slips, IT returns, business records)
Photographs of accident scene and injuries
Affidavit detailing relationship with deceased (in death cases)
important_notes: No time limitation for filing MACT claims — but file as early as possible for best evidence
No court fee required
You do not need a lawyer, but legal help is recommended
In hit-and-run cases where vehicle is unidentified, claim from the Solatium Fund under Section 161 MV Act — Rs. 50,000 minimum for death, Rs. 25,000 for disablement
Even pedestrians and passengers can file MACT claims
If vehicle was uninsured, the owner pays directly — insurance company is not involved
MACT can also award interest on compensation from the date of filing
grievance_mechanisms.legal_basis: Motor Vehicles Act 1988 — Sections 140 (no-fault liability), 161-163A (hit and run / Solatium Fund), 165-167 (MACT constitution), 166 (claims), 168 (just compensation)

grievance_mechanisms.title: Challenge False Drunk Driving Accusation
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you have been falsely accused of drunk driving or believe the breathalyzer test was inaccurate or the procedure was not followed correctly
steps: Step 1: Stay calm and cooperate — do not refuse the test (refusal is itself an offence under Section 185 MV Act)
Step 2: Ask for a second breathalyzer test — you have the right to demand a re-test
Step 3: If you dispute the breathalyzer result, demand a blood test at a government hospital — blood test is more accurate and admissible in court
Step 4: Note the officer's name, badge number, location, and time of the test
Step 5: Ask to see the calibration certificate of the breathalyzer device — uncalibrated devices give inaccurate results
Step 6: Do NOT sign any document admitting guilt
Step 7: If a challan is issued, collect all evidence — medical prescriptions (some medicines cause false positives), restaurant bills showing you didn't consume alcohol, witness statements
Step 8: File a grievance on the E-Challan portal with your evidence
Step 9: If challan is not cancelled, challenge it before the traffic magistrate court
Step 10: File an RTI for the breathalyzer's calibration certificate and the officer's duty log
important_notes: Blood alcohol limit in India: 30 mg per 100 ml of blood (Section 185 MV Act)
Penalty for drunk driving: Rs. 10,000 fine and/or 6 months imprisonment (first offence); Rs. 15,000 and/or 2 years (repeat)
Breathalyzer devices must be calibrated regularly — uncalibrated results are challengeable in court
Certain medications, mouthwashes, and medical conditions can cause false positives on breathalyzer
You have the right to demand a blood test as an alternative to breathalyzer
If police are demanding money to 'settle' instead of issuing a proper challan, report to ACB (1064)
grievance_mechanisms.legal_basis: Motor Vehicles Act 1988 — Section 185 (drunk driving), Section 203 (breathalyzer procedure); BNS Section 281 (rash driving)

grievance_mechanisms.title: Hit and Run — Victim's Rights and Compensation
grievance_mechanisms.when_to_use: When you or a family member is the victim of a hit-and-run accident where the driver fled the scene and the vehicle may or may not be identified
steps: Step 1: Call 112 immediately for emergency medical help
Step 2: If possible, note the vehicle number, colour, type, and direction it fled — ask bystanders and check for CCTV cameras nearby
Step 3: File an FIR at the nearest police station — mention it is a hit-and-run case
Step 4: Get a MLC (Medico-Legal Certificate) from the treating hospital
Step 5: If the vehicle is IDENTIFIED — file a MACT claim against the driver, owner, and insurance company (same as regular accident claim)
Step 6: If the vehicle is UNIDENTIFIED — apply for compensation from the Solatium Fund through the Claims Enquiry Officer of your district
Step 7: Solatium Fund compensation: minimum Rs. 50,000 for death, Rs. 25,000 for grievous hurt
Step 8: Police must investigate and try to identify the vehicle using CCTV, toll records, and witness statements
Step 9: Keep all medical bills and treatment records for compensation claim
important_notes: Hit and run is a serious criminal offence — BNS Section 106(2) provides up to 10 years imprisonment and Rs. 7 lakh fine
The driver who flees is criminally liable even if the accident was not their fault
Good Samaritans who help are protected by law — Section 134A MV Act
If vehicle is unidentified, the government pays from the Solatium Fund (Section 161 MV Act)
There is no time limitation for filing compensation claims
Police are required to submit Accident Information Report to MACT within 30 days
grievance_mechanisms.legal_basis: Motor Vehicles Act 1988 — Sections 134A, 161 (Solatium Fund), 163A (structured formula compensation), 166; BNS Section 106(2) (hit and run penalty)
